skill.name: hig-components-menus
skill.description: Apple Human Interface Guidelines guidance for menus, buttons, toolbars, menu bars, context menus, disclosure controls, and related platform behavior.
skill.summary: Design Apple-style menus, buttons, toolbars, and menu bar commands using HIG guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found them to be Markdown documentation false positives from Apple HIG links, image URLs, API names, and design terminology. No executable scripts, command execution, malware intent, credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts were found. The remaining risk is low because the skill contains external documentation links and an instruction to read a local design context file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The static network detections point to Markdown source links, Apple Developer documentation links, Apple documentation asset images, and attribution links. They are not network calls, fetch operations, credential endpoints, or exfiltration logic. A minor content risk remains because external links can change over time.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown links and image references, not executable network operations. The reviewed context shows documentation content from Apple HIG pages.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown Inline Code
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick detections are inline Markdown code spans for SF Symbols, SwiftUI, UIKit, and AppKit API names. I found no script file, shell invocation, command substitution, or user-controlled command execution in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines use Markdown backticks to format identifiers and file paths. There is no surrounding code context that could execute those strings.

low_findings.title: Blocker Keyword Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and C2 keyword hits appear in documentation URLs, image hashes, UI terms, and ordinary design prose. I did not find malware intent, obfuscation, credential handling, persistence, or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are Markdown front matter, image links, or UI documentation. The surrounding content is Apple design guidance rather than executable or operational code.

low_findings.title: Local Design Context File Instruction
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to check .claude/apple-design-context.md before asking questions. This is not malicious, but it is a low-risk filesystem behavior because it can cause reading local project context when the skill is active.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and limited to a named local design context file. I found no instruction to exfiltrate, modify, or hide the file contents.

content.user_title: Design Apple HIG Menus and Buttons
content.value_statement: Apple platform menu and button choices can be hard to align across devices. This skill gives focused HIG guidance for component choice, placement, labels, and platform behavior.
actual_capabilities: Recommends menu and button component types for Apple platform interfaces.
Explains placement, grouping, labeling, and visual hierarchy for menus and buttons.
Covers menu bars, context menus, Dock menus, edit menus, toolbars, action buttons, pop-up buttons, pull-down buttons, and disclosure controls.
Highlights platform-specific behavior for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, and visionOS.
Suggests keyboard shortcut considerations for macOS menu items and toolbar actions.
limitations: It is reference guidance and does not inspect a live app interface by itself.
The bundled Markdown references may lag behind current Apple documentation.
It focuses on menus and buttons, not complete app information architecture.
External Apple documentation links require internet access if the user wants to open sources.

use_cases.title: Choose the Right Component
use_cases.description: Decide whether an action belongs in a toolbar, context menu, menu bar, pop-up button, or pull-down button.

use_cases.title: Implement Platform Menus
use_cases.description: Map app commands to Apple platform conventions before building menus, toolbar items, and shortcuts.

use_cases.title: Review Interaction Specs
use_cases.description: Check that command placement, destructive actions, and hidden menus match expected user workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Component
prompt_templates.prompt: I need one control for [task]. The user can choose from [options]. Recommend the best Apple HIG menu or button component and explain why.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Toolbar
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this toolbar: [describe items and platform]. Identify which actions should stay visible, move to More, or move to menus.

prompt_templates.title: Map Menu Bar Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a macOS menu bar plan for [app]. Include standard menus, app-specific menus, command grouping, and shortcut guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Cross-Platform Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the menu and button behavior for [iOS/iPadOS/macOS/visionOS app]. Find inconsistencies with Apple HIG and suggest corrections.

output_examples.input: Should Delete appear in a context menu for selected files?
output_examples.output: Recommendation: include Delete only when it is relevant to the selected files. Place it near the end, mark it destructive, and keep the action available elsewhere.

output_examples.input: Where should Save, Export, and Share go in a macOS document app?
output_examples.output: Use File for Save and Export commands. Put Share where users expect collaboration or distribution actions, and keep frequent commands available from the toolbar when space allows.

output_examples.input: Should a toolbar contain every menu command?
output_examples.output: No. Use the toolbar for frequent actions. Keep all commands discoverable through menus, especially on macOS where the menu bar is the primary command interface.

best_practices: Start with the user task, then choose the smallest component that exposes the needed action clearly.
Keep destructive actions visible, clearly labeled, and separate from common safe actions.
Check platform differences before reusing one menu or toolbar layout across Apple devices.
anti_patterns: Do not hide the only path to an important command inside a context menu.
Do not put every command into a toolbar just because the command exists.
Do not use custom button styles when system styles communicate the action clearly.

faq.question: Does this skill write SwiftUI or UIKit code?
faq.answer: It provides design guidance. It can inform implementation prompts, but it is not a code generator by itself.

faq.question: Can it help with macOS menu bars?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for standard menu order, app menus, File, Edit, View, Window, Help, and custom menus.

faq.question: Does it cover context menus?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers when to use context menus, how to keep them short, and how to handle destructive actions.

faq.question: Can it compare pop-up buttons and pull-down buttons?
faq.answer: Yes. It distinguishes current-value selection from action lists and recommends the appropriate control.

faq.question: Does it support non-Apple platforms?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is specific to Apple Human Interface Guidelines and Apple platform conventions.

faq.question: Is internet access required?
faq.answer: No for the bundled guidance. Internet access is only needed to open the external Apple documentation links.
